coredata.dc:title: One-pot electrochemical synthesis of functionalized fluorescent carbon dots and their selective sensing for mercury ion
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Hou, Yuxin
Lu, Qiujun
Deng, Jianhui
Li, Haitao
Zhang, Youyu
coredata.dc:description: We propose a simple, economical, and one-pot method to synthesize water-soluble functionalized fluorescent carbon dots (C-Dots) through electrochemical carbonization of sodium citrate and urea. The as-prepared C-Dots have good photostability and exhibit a high quantum yield of 11.9%. The sizes of the C-Dots are mainly distributed in the range of 1.0–3.5nm with an average size of 2.4nm. It has been further used as a novel label-free sensing probe for selective detection of Hg2+ ions with detection limit as low as 3.3nM. The detection linear range is 0.01–10μM. The as-prepared C-Dots are also successfully applied for the determination of Hg2+ in real water samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Functionalized carbon dots
Electrochemical carbonization
One-pot synthesis
Fluorescent sensor
Mercury ions
Real water samples

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Possible mechanism of the FL response of C-Dots to Hg2+
Optimization of the Hg2+ detection conditions
Fluorescence response of C-Dots toward Hg2+

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 21375037 , 21275051 and 21475043 ), Scientific Research Fund of Hunan Provincial Science and Technology Department and Education Department ( 13JJ2020 , 12A084 ), and Doctoral Fund of Ministry of Education of China (No.: 20134306110006 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the Hg2+ and Cys detection mechanism using the carbon dots.
(A) TEM image of the C-Dots. Inset: the particle size distribution. (B) FT-IR spectrum of the C-Dots.

(A) UV/vis absorption spectrum (dotted line) and FL spectra (solid lines) of the obtained C-Dots. Inset: photograph of the obtained C-Dots under illumination of UV (365nm) light. (B) Normalized FL emission spectra with increasingly longer excitation wavelengths (in 20nm increments starting from 300) of C-Dots. (For interpretation of the references to color in the text, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(A) Normalized FL intensity of C-Dots at different pH. (B) FL response of C-Dots in the presence of different concentrations of NaCl. (C) Normalized FL intensity of C-Dots at 433nm at different time of illumination of 351nm. (D) Normalized FL intensity of C-Dots after several months. (I 0 and I correspond to the FL intensity of C-Dots at 433nm in the absence and presence of NaCl, respectively. Excitation wavelength is 351nm.)

FL emission spectra of the C-Dots in the absence (a) and presence (c) of 50μM Hg2+ and FL restoration of the C-Dots/Hg2+ system in the presence of 0.1mM Cys (b). (Performed in pH 6.0, 10mM PBS. Excitation wavelength is 351nm).

The plot of the C-Dots FL-quenched efficiency (I 0 − I)/I 0 at 433nm by 50μM Hg2+ versus different pHs (A) and versus different time (B). (Performed in pH 6.0, 10mM PBS; I 0 and I correspond to the FL intensity of the C-Dots at 433nm in the absence and presence of Hg2+, respectively. Excitation wavelength is 351nm).

FL emission spectra of C-Dots in the presence of different concentrations of Hg2+ (from top to bottom, 0, 0.01, 0.1, 1, 2, 4, 5, 6, 8, 10, 20, and 50μM). Inset shows the dependence of (I 0 − I)/I 0 on the concentrations of Hg2+ within the range of 0–10μM. (Performed in pH 6.0, 10mM sodium phosphate buffer; I 0 and I correspond to the FL intensity of the C-Dots at 433nm in the absence and presence of Hg2+, respectively. Excitation wavelength is 351nm).

FL-quenched efficiency (I 0 − I)/I 0 of C-Dots in the presence of different metal ions. (Performed in pH 6.0, 10mM PBS; the concentration of metal ions is 10μM; I 0 and I correspond to the FL intensity of C-Dots at 433nm in the absence and presence of metal ions, respectively. Excitation wavelength is 351nm).
Determination of Hg2+ in natural water samples.

head.ce:title: One-pot electrochemical synthesis of functionalized fluorescent carbon dots and their selective sensing for mercury ion

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Chemical Biology and Traditional Chinese Medicine Research (Ministry of Education), College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hunan Normal University, Changsha 410081, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Chemical Biology and Traditional Chinese Medicine Research (Ministry of Education)
College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We propose a simple, economical, and one-pot method to synthesize water-soluble functionalized fluorescent carbon dots (C-Dots) through electrochemical carbonization of sodium citrate and urea. The as-prepared C-Dots have good photostability and exhibit a high quantum yield of 11.9%. The sizes of the C-Dots are mainly distributed in the range of 1.0–3.5nm with an average size of 2.4nm. It has been further used as a novel label-free sensing probe for selective detection of Hg2+ ions with detection limit as low as 3.3nM. The detection linear range is 0.01–10μM. The as-prepared C-Dots are also successfully applied for the determination of Hg2+ in real water samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescent nanostructures are very attractive because of their promised applications in optoelectronic devices, biology labeling and biomedicine [1]. In the development of fluorescent nanomaterials, the discovery of semiconductor quantum dots (QDs) is considered as a major milestone. Semiconductor QDs usually have size-tunable and narrow emission spectra, high photostability, and resistance to metabolic degradation in bioapplications [2]. However, most of the high-performance QDs are composed of toxic heavy metal elements such as cadmium, which limit their use [3]. Thus, the development of non-toxic or low toxic fluorescent nanomaterials instead of semiconductor QDs is very necessary.

Carbon dots (C-Dots) have attracted tremendous attention, owing to their outstanding optical properties, low toxicity, good biocompatibility and robust chemical inertness [4]. A variety of methods have been developed to prepare fluorescent C-Dots, such as arc discharge [5], laser ablation [6], oxidative acid treatment [7], hydrothermal synthesis [8], electrochemical etching [9], pyrolysis [10], and microwave-assisted synthesis [11]. Sun et al. [6] reported a synthesis route toward fluorescent C-Dots via laser ablation of graphite powder in the presence of water vapor with argon as carrier gas. Liu et al. [7] reported a preparation method of C-Dots from the combustion soot of candles by treating with an oxidative acid and the subsequent purification using polyacrylamide gel electrophoresis (PAGE). Pan et al. [10] developed a one-step synthetic method for highly C-Dots by pyrolysis of ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA) salts under a nitrogen atmosphere. Those methods mentioned above are available for the synthesis of C-Dots, however, most of prepared C-Dots have a low degree of functionality, which result in a poor interaction with analytes, consequently limit their application in the analytical chemistry. Recently, some efforts have been made on controlling the surface properties and functionalizing of C-Dots. Zhu et al. [12] integrated C-Dots coated with a specific organic molecule N-(2-aminoethyl)-N,N,N′tris(pyridin-2-ylmethyl)ethane-1,2-diamine (AE-TPEA) and CdSe/ZnS QDs to develop a ratiometric strategy for intracellular sensing of Cu2+ ions. Yu et al. [13] constructed a fluorescence resonance energy transfer system by integrating C-Dots with a NO recognition element phenylenediamine-containing naphthalimide for NO detection. Sun et al. [14] modified C-Dots with a cyanine dye which had a unique reactivity to bisulfite to develop a fluorescence (FL) nanoprobe for selective and quantitative detection of SO2. These C-Dots-based materials are successful in constructing fluorescent nanosensors. However, their preparations usually require specific receptors and complicated surface modifications. Thus, developing simple and effective methods for rapid fabrication of specially functionalized C-Dots is highly desired.

Herein, we propose a simple, economical, and one-pot method to synthesize water-soluble functional fluorescent C-Dots through electrochemical carbonization of sodium citrate and urea. Compared with the previous work, the reaction time of proposed method is shorter and this method does not need expensive reagents, strong acid, high temperature, and further surface modification. Considering the mercury ion (Hg2+) is a toxic heavy metal ion, and the Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) standard for the maximum allowable level of Hg2+ in drinking water is only 2ppb [15]. The detection of Hg2+ concentration in the environmental monitoring system is important. Therefore, we design a fluorescence analysis method for high sensitive detection of Hg2+ based on as-prepared functionalized C-Dots. It is found that the as-prepared C-Dots can serve as a very effective fluorescent probe for label-free, sensitive, and selective detection of Hg2+.

Sodium citrate, urea, sodium dihydrogen phosphate, disodium hydrogen phosphate, were obtained from Sinopharm Chem. Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All chemicals were used as received without any further purification. Ultrapure water (18.2mΩ; Millpore Co., USA) was used throughout the experiment.

Transmission electron microscopy images of C-Dots were obtained using a JEOL-1230 transmission electron microscope (JEOL, Japan). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrum in the 4000–400cm−1 region was recorded on a Nicolet Nexus 670 FT-IR spectroscope (Nicolet Instrument Co., USA). Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) spectra were collected using a UV-2450 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu Co., Japan). Fluorescence spectra were obtained using a F-4500 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi Ltd., Japan).

C-Dots were synthesized through a simple, convenient and one-step electrochemical method. Sodium citrate and urea with appropriate proportion were added to ultrapure water (10mL) to form a transparent solution under stirring. Two platinum sheets (1.5×2cm2) were employed as the positive and negative electrodes, respectively, and the distance between the two platinum sheets was about 1cm. The reaction proceeded for about 1h at 5V potential (DC) until the transparent solution turned brown. The product solution was dialyzed against ultrapure water through a dialysis membrane (with 1000Da MWCO) for about 6h to obtain the fluorescent C-Dots.

The detection of Hg2+ was performed at room temperature in pH 6.0, 10mM phosphate buffer solution (PBS). 20μL of C-Dots solution was added into 880μL of PBS buffer, followed by the addition of 100μL different concentrations of Hg2+. The FL emission spectra were recorded after reaction for 15min at room temperature.

In order to obtain C-Dots with excellent fluorescence properties, we investigated the effect of the ratio of sodium citrate and urea. Different C-Dots were synthesized at the ratio of sodium citrate and urea 1:3, 3:1 and 2:2, respectively. The FL quantum yield was detected with quinine sulfate solution (quantum yield 54%) as reference. The FL quantum yield of the C-Dots synthesized by the ratio of sodium citrate and urea at 1:3, 3:1 and 2:2 are 11.9%, 2.6% and 3.0%, respectively. The maximum excitation wavelength and emission wavelength of these C-Dots are nearly unchanged, and they both have the excitation-wavelength-dependent FL properties (Figs. S1 and S2). Among these different C-Dots, we had chosen the optimum C-Dots with a high quantum yield of 11.9% which were synthesized at the ratio of sodium citrate and urea 1:3 as fluorescent probe for further application.

The morphology of the as-prepared C-Dots was characterized using transmission electron microscopy (TEM). As shown in Fig. 1 A, the TEM image of the C-Dots reveals that the as-prepared C-Dots are well-dispersed. The sizes of C-Dots are mainly distributed in the range of 1.0–3.5nm with an average size of 2.4nm (inset in Fig. 1A). The functional groups on surface of C-Dots were then investigated using FT-IR spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. 1B, the FT-IR spectrum of C-Dots exhibits distinct absorption bands at 3261, 3336, 3425cm−1, which can be attributed to stretching vibrations of OH and NH. The absorption band at 1668cm−1 is assigned to the vibration of CO. The high-intensity peaks at 1596 and 1398cm−1 correspond to the asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibrations of COO−, respectively [16]. The above observations confirm that the as-synthesized nanoparticles functionalized by hydroxyl, carboxyl and amino groups can improve the stability and hydrophilicity of the C-Dots in an aqueous system.

The optical properties of the as-prepared C-Dots were investigated by the UV–vis absorption and the FL spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. 2 A, C-Dots aqueous solution has a broad absorption spectrum (dotted line). The maximum excitation wavelength and emission wavelength of the C-Dots aqueous solution are 351 and 433nm, respectively (solid lines). Very bright blue luminescence under the illumination of UV (365nm) light of the C-Dots solution can be clearly seen in the inset of Fig. 2A. The C-Dots solution exhibits excitation-wavelength-dependent FL properties with emission peaks ranging from 410nm to 517nm at excitation from 300nm to 460nm (Fig. 2B). The behavior is considered to be related to different sizes and a distribution of different surface states of carbon nanoparticles [4]. The FL quantum yield of C-Dots is measured to be about 11.9% with quinine sulfate in 0.1M sulfuric acid solution (quantum yield 54%) as reference. This quantum yield value is comparable to previous reports [17].

To investigate the stability, FL intensity of the C-Dots solution under extreme pH, high ionic strengths or long time illumination was measured. Fig. 3 A shows the FL response of C-Dots at different pH values. The FL of the C-Dots is strong and stable in a wide range of pH values (3–10). Although it is slightly increased at pH>10, this result indicates that the as-prepared C-Dots still show good stability in strong acid or strong alkaline conditions. We also investigated the effect of ionic strength on the stability of C-Dots. As shown in Fig. 3B, the FL intensity of C-Dots at 433nm does not change obviously even when the concentration of NaCl is as high as 1M. We then explored the resistance to photobleaching of such C-Dots through the study of UV light illumination. It is seen that the FL intensity only decreases about 4% under continuous UV light (351nm) illumination for 1h (Fig. 3C). Furthermore, it is found that the C-Dots still exhibit good photostability when stored at room temperature for about three months (Fig. 3D). These results reveal that the C-Dots have good photostability, which possess great potential in the actual sensing application.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible mechanism of the FL response of C-Dots to Hg2+
ce:section.ce:para: As mentioned above, the FT-IR spectrum indicates that the synthesized nanoparticles function with hydroxyl, carboxyl and amino. Moreover, these functional groups can chelate with metal ions to form stable chelate complexes. Lu et al. [16] reported Hg2+ had a stronger affinity toward the carboxylic group. Enlightened by this investigation, we designed an unmodified C-Dots FL sensor for label-free detection of Hg2+. The approach is depicted in Scheme 1 . C-Dots were synthesized from sodium citrate and urea through a one-step electrochemical carbonization method. The free C-Dots showed strong blue FL under the illumination of ultraviolet light (351nm). After the addition of Hg2+, the carboxylic group on the C-Dots surface could chelate with Hg2+, which accelerated the non-radiative recombination of the excitons through an effective electron transfer process, leading to a substantial decrease of the FL in intensity. The feasibility of Hg2+ detection was explored. As shown in Fig. 4 , C-Dots solution in the absence of Hg2+ exhibits a strong PL (curve a). In contrast, the presence of 50μM Hg2+ leads to an obvious decrease of FL in intensity (curve c). The fluorescence quenching may be due to Hg2+-induced the aggregation of C-Dots or the chelation of Hg2+. In order to investigate whether the aggregation of C-Dots occurs after the addition of Hg2+, the morphology of C-Dots/Hg2+ is further studied by TEM (Fig. S3). The result indicates that the C-Dots/Hg2+ are well-dispersed and the aggregation of C-Dots does not significantly occur. Therefore, Hg2+ can effectively quench the FL of C-Dots that may be induced by the chelation of Hg2+ with the carboxylate groups of C-Dots through the electron transfer process [8]. Meanwhile, upon addition of cysteine (Cys) to the solution of C-Dots/Hg2+, the quenched FL of C-Dots is recovered immediately (curve b). The FL enhancement of C-Dots/Hg2+ solution may be attributable to the formation of Hg2+–S between Hg2+ and Cys which reduces the chelation of Hg2+ and C-Dots. It is found that other biothiols, such as glutathione, homocyateine have the same effect on the FL recovery of the C-Dots/Hg2+ system. Therefore, it is difficult to distinguish these biothiols (cysteine, homocyateine, glutathione, etc.). In addition, the intrinsic spectral peak of solution is unchanged in comparison with that of free C-Dots. The results show that this scheme is feasible.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of the Hg2+ detection conditions
ce:section.ce:para: In order to obtain better detection sensitivity, the optimization of experimental conditions such as solution pH value, action time and so on, is very necessary. The pH effect was studied by monitoring the dependence of the quenched FL efficiency (I 0 − I)/I 0 on the pH values in the 10mM PBS at pH 3.0, 4.0, 5.0, 6.0, 7.0, 8.0, 9.0. As shown in Fig. 5 A, the FL-quenched efficiency reaches the highest value at pH 6.0. Therefore, the buffer of pH 6.0 is used in this experiment. The dependence of FL-quenched efficiency by Hg2+ on the reaction time was also studied (Fig. 5B). The result reveals that the FL-quenched efficiency reaches a constant value within 1min after the addition of Hg2+ (50μM), which indicates that the quenching kinetic is fairly fast.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence response of C-Dots toward Hg2+
ce:section.ce:para: We have evaluated the analytical performance of the detection system for quantitative detection of Hg2+ under the optimized conditions. As shown in Fig. 6 , the FL intensity of C-Dots at 433nm decreases gradually with increasing concentration of Hg2+. Inset presents the FL-quenched efficiency (I 0 − I)/I 0 versus the concentration of Hg2+. A good linear correlation (R 2 =0.997) is observed over the concentration range of 0.01–10μM and the detection limit is estimated to be 3.3nM at a signal-to-noise ratio of 3, which is comparable to previously reported values (Table S1) and lower than the maximum level for mercury in drinking water permitted by the US Environmental Protection Agency (2ppb, 10nM) [15].

Besides sensitivity, selectivity is another important parameter to evaluate the performance of the sensing system. Therefore, we examined the FL intensity changes in the presence of representative metal ions under the same conditions, including Hg2+, Ca2+, Cu2+, Al3+, Ba2+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Pb2+, Co2+, Zn2+, Fe3+ and Mg2+, as shown in Fig. 7 . It is noticeable that only Hg2+ can induce a substantial decrease in the FL intensity of C-Dots, in contrast, no tremendous decrease is observed by adding other ions into the C-Dots solution, implying that C-Dots can be used to selectively detect Hg2+ in an aqueous solution. The outstanding selectivity and specificity can be probably attributed to that Hg2+ has a stronger affinity toward the carboxylic group on the C-Dots surface than other metal ions.

The feasibility of C-Dots for detecting Hg2+ in real samples was explored by natural water (river water, fountain water and tap water). The water samples were spiked with standard solutions containing different concentrations of Hg2+. The results are summarized in Table 1 . For the three samples, they both have similar results and good recovery rates range from 95.69 to 105.56%. These results imply that the as-prepared C-Dots are likely to be capable of practically useful Hg2+ detection upon further development.

In summary, electrochemical carbonization of sodium citrate and urea has been proven to be an effective strategy for producing functionalized C-Dots. Furthermore, no further chemical modification of C-Dots is required, which offers the advantages of simplicity and cost efficiency. The as-prepared C-Dots have good photostability and have been further used as a novel sensing probe for label-free, sensitive detection of Hg2+. This sensing system also possesses high selectivity toward Hg2+ analysis and has been successfully used for the analysis of natural water sample. Our method possesses of the advantages of good selectivity, high sensitivity, easily operation, which will play an important role in biological and environmental applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21375037, 21275051 and 21475043), Scientific Research Fund of Hunan Provincial Science and Technology Department and Education Department (13JJ2020, 12A084), and Doctoral Fund of Ministry of Education of China (No.: 20134306110006).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.aca.2015.01.039.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following are Supplementary data to this article:
